sections.heading: Complications
sections.content: Complication: Brain edema and mass effect. Management: Corticosteroid therapy with careful monitoring; lowest dose for shortest time; patients with extensive mass effect should receive steroids for at least 24 hours before RT; consider H2 blockers or PPI for high GI risk patients. Complication: Seizures. Management: Seizure prophylaxis NOT recommended as routine in asymptomatic patients; reasonable to consider perioperatively; avoid enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine); prefer non-EIAEDs (levetiracetam, topiramate, valproic acid, lacosamide). Complication: Pseudoprogression. Management: Occurs in first 3 months or longer after completion of RT; more often in MGMT promoter methylated tumors; biopsy, MR spectroscopy, MR perfusion, or brain PET/CT can differentiate from true progression. Complication: Radiation necrosis. Management: Tends to be detected between 6 and 24 months following RT treatment; consider bevacizumab if symptoms do not resolve with corticosteroids; consider hyperbaric oxygen; consider LITT. Complication: Hydrocephalus. Management: VP shunt placement if needed; neurosurgical evaluation. Complication: Spinal cord compression. Management: Medical emergency; steroids (minimum 4 mg dexamethasone every 6 hours); decompressive surgery followed by RT is preferred (category 1); primary RT for radiosensitive tumors. Complication: Endocrinopathies. Management: Common in brain tumor patients; evaluate hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, thyroid, and gonad function; long-term monitoring of hypothalamic-pituitary and adrenal axis may be considered for patients who received prior RT. Complication: Neurocognitive dysfunction. Management: Up to 90% of individuals with supratentorial brain tumors experience some degree; neuropsychological evaluation is gold standard; consider neuropsychological evaluation as needed based on physician assessment. Complication: Cranial nerve palsies. Management: Depends on location and cause; surgical evaluation for structural lesions. Complication: Hearing loss (NF2-related). Management: Multidisciplinary care including otologists; bevacizumab results in hearing improvement in 36% of NF2-SWN patients; CI or ABI may be considered; auditory rehabilitation. Complication: Leptomeningeal disease. Management: Prognosis-dependent treatment; CSF analysis; systemic therapy, intra-CSF therapy, and/or RT as appropriate. Complication: Myelosuppression (especially neutropenia). Management: Monitor CBC; dose modifications as needed; PCV regimen associated with significant myelosuppression; 70% of patients in EORTC 26951 did not complete planned 6 cycles of PCV. Complication: Nephrotoxicity from high-dose methotrexate. Management: Consider glucarpidase (carboxypeptidase G2) for prolonged methotrexate clearance due to methotrexate-induced renal toxicity. Complication: Fatigue. Management: Screen for underlying medical causes; physical exercise has best evidence for prevention and treatment; consider psychostimulants for attention improvement. Complication: Hepatotoxicity from bevacizumab. Management: Monitor liver function; hold if significant elevation. Complication: Stomatitis from temozolomide. Management: Oral care protocols; dose modifications as needed. Complication: Immune-related adverse events. Management: Monitor for organ-specific toxicities; corticosteroids as needed; ipilimumab/nivolumab combination for melanoma brain metastases associated with grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events in just over half of patients. Complication: Skin toxicity from BRAF/MEK inhibitors. Management: Dermatologic monitoring; supportive care. Complication: Pulmonary toxicity from ALK inhibitors. Management: Monitor for pulmonary adverse events; 180 mg once daily with 7-day lead-in at 90 mg for brigatinib to reduce early-onset moderate to severe pulmonary adverse events. Complication: GI toxicity from EGFR TKIs. Management: Supportive care; dose modifications. Complication: Neurologic deficits. Management: Careful surgical planning; use of surgical adjuncts (intraoperative microscope, stereotactic image guidance, fMRI, awake craniotomy, motor/speech mapping). Complication: Postoperative seizures. Management: Perioperative seizure prophylaxis reasonable. Complication: Infection. Management: Standard perioperative antibiotics; monitor for signs of infection. Complication: Wound healing complications. Management: Watertight dural closure when carmustine wafers used; sufficient steroids and antiepileptics in perioperative period. Complication: Neurocognitive decline. Management: Use cogniti...

cards.name: Brain edema and mass effect
cards.detail: Corticosteroid therapy with careful monitoring; lowest dose for shortest time; patients with extensive mass effect should receive steroids for at least 24 hours before RT; consider H2 blockers or PPI for high GI risk patients

cards.name: Seizures
cards.detail: Seizure prophylaxis NOT recommended as routine in asymptomatic patients; reasonable to consider perioperatively; avoid enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine); prefer non-EIAEDs (levetiracetam, topiramate, valproic acid, lacosamide)

cards.name: Pseudoprogression
cards.detail: Occurs in first 3 months or longer after completion of RT; more often in MGMT promoter methylated tumors; biopsy, MR spectroscopy, MR perfusion, or brain PET/CT can differentiate from true progression

cards.name: Radiation necrosis
cards.detail: Tends to be detected between 6 and 24 months following RT treatment; consider bevacizumab if symptoms do not resolve with corticosteroids; consider hyperbaric oxygen; consider LITT

cards.name: Hydrocephalus
cards.detail: VP shunt placement if needed; neurosurgical evaluation

cards.name: Spinal cord compression
cards.detail: Medical emergency; steroids (minimum 4 mg dexamethasone every 6 hours); decompressive surgery followed by RT is preferred (category 1); primary RT for radiosensitive tumors

cards.name: Endocrinopathies
cards.detail: Common in brain tumor patients; evaluate hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, thyroid, and gonad function; long-term monitoring of hypothalamic-pituitary and adrenal axis may be considered for patients who received prior RT

cards.name: Neurocognitive dysfunction
cards.detail: Up to 90% of individuals with supratentorial brain tumors experience some degree; neuropsychological evaluation is gold standard; consider neuropsychological evaluation as needed based on physician assessment

cards.name: Cranial nerve palsies
cards.detail: Depends on location and cause; surgical evaluation for structural lesions

cards.name: Hearing loss (NF2-related)
cards.detail: Multidisciplinary care including otologists; bevacizumab results in hearing improvement in 36% of NF2-SWN patients; CI or ABI may be considered; auditory rehabilitation

cards.name: Leptomeningeal disease
cards.detail: Prognosis-dependent treatment; CSF analysis; systemic therapy, intra-CSF therapy, and/or RT as appropriate

cards.name: Myelosuppression (especially neutropenia)
cards.detail: Monitor CBC; dose modifications as needed; PCV regimen associated with significant myelosuppression; 70% of patients in EORTC 26951 did not complete planned 6 cycles of PCV

cards.name: Nephrotoxicity from high-dose methotrexate
cards.detail: Consider glucarpidase (carboxypeptidase G2) for prolonged methotrexate clearance due to methotrexate-induced renal toxicity

cards.name: Fatigue
cards.detail: Screen for underlying medical causes; physical exercise has best evidence for prevention and treatment; consider psychostimulants for attention improvement

cards.name: Hepatotoxicity from bevacizumab
cards.detail: Monitor liver function; hold if significant elevation

cards.name: Stomatitis from temozolomide
cards.detail: Oral care protocols; dose modifications as needed

cards.name: Immune-related adverse events
cards.detail: Monitor for organ-specific toxicities; corticosteroids as needed; ipilimumab/nivolumab combination for melanoma brain metastases associated with grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events in just over half of patients

cards.name: Skin toxicity from BRAF/MEK inhibitors
cards.detail: Dermatologic monitoring; supportive care

cards.name: Pulmonary toxicity from ALK inhibitors
cards.detail: Monitor for pulmonary adverse events; 180 mg once daily with 7-day lead-in at 90 mg for brigatinib to reduce early-onset moderate to severe pulmonary adverse events

cards.name: GI toxicity from EGFR TKIs
cards.detail: Supportive care; dose modifications

cards.name: Neurologic deficits
cards.detail: Careful surgical planning; use of surgical adjuncts (intraoperative microscope, stereotactic image guidance, fMRI, awake craniotomy, motor/speech mapping)

cards.name: Postoperative seizures
cards.detail: Perioperative seizure prophylaxis reasonable

cards.name: Infection
cards.detail: Standard perioperative antibiotics; monitor for signs of infection

cards.name: Wound healing complications
cards.detail: Watertight dural closure when carmustine wafers used; sufficient steroids and antiepileptics in perioperative period

cards.name: Neurocognitive decline
cards.detail: Use cogniti...
